From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Honorific prefix

Not to be confused with [The Honourable](/wiki/The_Honourable "The Honourable"), [The Most Honourable](/wiki/The_Most_Honourable "The Most Honourable"), or [The Much Honoured](/wiki/The_Much_Honoured "The Much Honoured").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Portrait_of_The_Right_Honourable_Sir_George_Cornewall_Lewis%2C_Bart_%284671604%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_The_Right_Honourable_Sir_George_Cornewall_Lewis%2C_Bart_%284671604%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_The_Right_Honourable_Sir_George_Cornewall_Lewis,_Bart_(4671604)_(cropped).jpg)

This [engraving](/wiki/Stipple_engraving "Stipple engraving") of [George Cornewall Lewis](/wiki/George_Cornewall_Lewis "George Cornewall Lewis") includes *The Right Honourable* in its caption, reflecting the [Home Secretary](/wiki/Home_Secretary "Home Secretary") position he held at the time of its creation.

***The Right Honourable*** ([abbreviation](/wiki/Abbreviation "Abbreviation"): **The Rt Hon.** or variations) is an [honorific](/wiki/Honorific "Honorific") [style](/wiki/Style_(form_of_address) "Style (form of address)") traditionally applied to certain persons and collective bodies in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), the former [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), and the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"). The term is predominantly used today as a style associated with the holding of certain senior public offices in the United Kingdom, [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), and, to a lesser extent, [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal").

*Right* in this context is an [adverb](/wiki/Adverb "Adverb") meaning 'very' or 'fully'.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Grammatically, *The Right Honourable* is an [adjectival phrase](/wiki/Adjectival_phrase "Adjectival phrase") which gives information about a person.[[2]](#cite_note-2) As such, it is not considered correct to apply it in direct address, nor to use it on its own as a title in place of a name; but rather it is used in the [third person](/wiki/Grammatical_person "Grammatical person") along with a name or noun to be modified.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMontague-Smith197933–35,_80–82-3)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHickey2013365–372,_378–379,_396–402-4)[[a]](#cite_note-5)

*Right* may be abbreviated to *Rt*, and *Honourable* to *Hon.*, or both. *The* is sometimes dropped in written abbreviated form, but is always pronounced.

## Countries with common or current usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Countries with common or current usage")]

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

#### Entitlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Entitlement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Victoria_Privy_Council_%28Wilke%29.jpg/330px-Victoria_Privy_Council_%28Wilke%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victoria_Privy_Council_(Wilke).jpg)

[Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") holding a meeting of her Privy Council. All privy counsellors are styled *Right Honourable* (unless they are personally entitled to a higher style).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Lord_Montagu_of_Beaulieu_15_Allan_Waren.jpg/250px-Lord_Montagu_of_Beaulieu_15_Allan_Waren.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lord_Montagu_of_Beaulieu_15_Allan_Waren.jpg)

The [parliamentary robes](/wiki/Parliamentary_robes "Parliamentary robes") of a baron, worn by [Lord Montagu of Beaulieu](/wiki/Edward_Douglas-Scott-Montagu,_3rd_Baron_Montagu_of_Beaulieu "Edward Douglas-Scott-Montagu, 3rd Baron Montagu of Beaulieu"). Peers of the rank of baron, viscount or earl are entitled to the style *Right Honourable*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b9/Gavyn_Arthur.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gavyn_Arthur.jpg)

[Gavyn Arthur](/wiki/Gavyn_Arthur_(judge) "Gavyn Arthur (judge)"), the 675th [Lord Mayor of London](/wiki/Lord_Mayor_of_London "Lord Mayor of London"), was entitled to be styled "The Right Honourable Lord Mayor of London" during his year in office.

According to the [British government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom"),[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerrers1989c.1116WA,_"The_Lord_Mayors_of_Belfast_and_Cardiff_and_the_Lord_Provost_of_Glasgow_are_entitled_to_the_style_'Right_Honourable'_by_express_grant_of_the_Sovereign._The_style_is_also_taken_by_Privy_Counsellors,_Peers_below_the_rank_of_Marquess_(which_includes_ladies_who_are_Peers_in_their_own_right),_the_Lord_Mayors_of_London_and_York_and_the_Lord_Provost_of_Edinburgh_by_ancient_prescriptive_usage."-6) the following persons are entitled to be styled *Right Honourable*:

Members of the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council_(United_Kingdom) "Privy Council (United Kingdom)")
:   The Privy Council is notionally the body of formal advisers to the sovereign. Members of the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom") (including the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom")), senior politicians, and some few other officials are appointed as members for life, and are personally entitled to be styled *Right Honourable* thereafter.[[6]](#cite_note-7)[[7]](#cite_note-8)

[Peers](/wiki/Peerage_of_the_United_Kingdom "Peerage of the United Kingdom") below the rank of [marquess](/wiki/Marquess "Marquess")
:   [Earls and countesses](/wiki/Earl "Earl"),[[8]](#cite_note-debretts-earls-9) [viscounts and viscountesses](/wiki/Viscount_(United_Kingdom) "Viscount (United Kingdom)")[[9]](#cite_note-debretts-viscounts-10) and [barons and baronesses](/wiki/Baron "Baron")[[10]](#cite_note-debretts-barons-11) who hold a [substantive title](/wiki/Substantive_title "Substantive title") (whether hereditary or for life) are personally entitled to the style *Right Honourable*.[[11]](#cite_note-britannica-12) A peer's wife or widow also has a legal right to the style of her husband.

The [lord mayors](/wiki/Lord_mayor "Lord mayor") and [lord provosts](/wiki/Lord_provost "Lord provost") of certain cities ([ex officio](/wiki/Ex_officio_member "Ex officio member"))
:   The lord mayors of London, Cardiff, Belfast, Bristol and York and the lord provosts of Edinburgh and Glasgow are styled *Right Honourable* while in office. However, the title is attached to the office, not to the name of the person.

#### Privy counsellors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Privy counsellors")]

Privy counsellors are appointed by the sovereign on the advice of the prime minister, and remain members for life unless they resign or are expelled. In practice, membership of the privy council is granted to:

* all members of the Cabinet (itself technically a committee of the Privy Council), and certain other senior ministers in the government;
* senior members of the [Shadow Cabinet](/wiki/Official_Opposition_Shadow_Cabinet_(United_Kingdom) "Official Opposition Shadow Cabinet (United Kingdom)"), the leaders of the major political parties in parliament, and the [Speaker of the House of Commons](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_House_of_Commons_(United_Kingdom) "Speaker of the House of Commons (United Kingdom)");
* the first ministers of Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland, and the leader of the largest opposition party in the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament");
* the two archbishops of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), who sit in the House of Lords ex officio;
* senior judges, who fulfil the judicial functions of the Privy Council;
* senior representatives of the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") nations; and
* senior members of the [royal family](/wiki/British_royal_family "British royal family").

A large proportion of the former and current prominent politicians of the United Kingdom are thus entitled to be styled *Right Honourable*.

No new appointments have been made to the [Privy Council of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_Northern_Ireland "Privy Council of Northern Ireland") since 1971, but surviving appointed members remain entitled to the style. Non-British Commonwealth-citizen judges appointed to the [Judicial Committee of the Privy Council](/wiki/Judicial_Committee_of_the_Privy_Council "Judicial Committee of the Privy Council") are also entitled to the style, although the appellation may be ignored in the judge's home country.

It is the current practice of the House of Lords[[12]](#cite_note-13) and the College of Arms to apply the style *Right Honourable* to privy counsellors only.[[13]](#cite_note-home-office-2021-14)

#### Peers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Peers")]

All holders of a substantive peerage below the rank of marquess are entitled to be styled *Right Honourable*,[[b]](#cite_note-21) as are their wives and widows.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, a peer's heir who uses a [courtesy title](/wiki/Courtesy_titles_in_the_United_Kingdom "Courtesy titles in the United Kingdom") is not accorded the corresponding style.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Peers above the rank of earl are entitled to different styles: [dukes and duchesses](/wiki/Duke "Duke") are styled *[The Most Noble](/wiki/The_Most_Noble "The Most Noble")* or [*His* or *Her Grace*](/wiki/Grace_(style) "Grace (style)"), and [marquesses and marchionesses](/wiki/Marquess "Marquess") are styled as *[The Most Honourable](/wiki/The_Most_Honourable "The Most Honourable")*.

In order to differentiate peers who are also members of the Privy Council—and therefore entitled to a style in both capacities—from peers who are not, the [post-nominal letters](/wiki/Post-nominal_letters "Post-nominal letters") *[PC](/wiki/Privy_Council_(United_Kingdom) "Privy Council (United Kingdom)")* can be used to identify the privy counsellors.[[c]](#cite_note-29) This applies to peers of all rank, as a holder of a dukedom or marquessate who becomes a Privy Counsellor retains their higher style and so could not be identified without the letters. In practice, in contexts where there might be confusion, official publications use the style *Right Honourable* exclusively to identify privy counsellors.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerrers1989cc._1117WA–1118WA,_"For_official_purposes,_the_style_is_omitted_in_many_circumstances,_owing_to_the_need_for_brevity,_clarity_or_informality,_as_the_occasion_dictates._For_example,_the_style_is_omitted_in_lists_of_Ministers_for_Peers_who_fall_below_the_rank_of_Marquess_and_who_are_not_Privy_Counsellors_in_order_to_distinguish_them_from_Peers_who_are_Privy_Counsellors."-30)[[13]](#cite_note-home-office-2021-14)

#### Lord mayors, lord provosts and other officers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Lord mayors, lord provosts and other officers")]

The lord mayors of London, Cardiff, Belfast and York; and the lord provosts of Edinburgh and Glasgow are all entitled to be styled *Right Honourable* while in office. The lord mayors of Belfast and Cardiff are so entitled by an explicit grant from the sovereign, and the others through ancient custom.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerrers1989c.1116WA,_"The_Lord_Mayors_of_Belfast_and_Cardiff_and_the_Lord_Provost_of_Glasgow_are_entitled_to_the_style_'Right_Honourable'_by_express_grant_of_the_Sovereign._The_style_is_also_taken_by_Privy_Counsellors,_Peers_below_the_rank_of_Marquess_(which_includes_ladies_who_are_Peers_in_their_own_right),_the_Lord_Mayors_of_London_and_York_and_the_Lord_Provost_of_Edinburgh_by_ancient_prescriptive_usage."-6) The style is used with the name of the office, not the personal name of the office-holder, e.g. "The Right Honourable Lord Mayor of London" or "The Right Honourable the Lord Provost of the City of Edinburgh".[[28]](#cite_note-dca-2003-31)

Other lord mayors may be styled *[Right Worshipful](/wiki/Worship_(style) "Worship (style)")*, and other lord provosts do not use a style. By the 1920s, a number of city mayors, including the Lord Mayor of Leeds,[[29]](#cite_note-32) were unofficially using the style *Right Honourable*, and the matter was consequently raised in parliament. The Lord Mayor of Bristol at present still uses the style *Right Honourable*, without official permission.[[30]](#cite_note-33)[[31]](#cite_note-34) In guidance issued in June 2003, the [Crown Office](/wiki/Crown_Office_in_Chancery "Crown Office in Chancery") recommended that the lord provosts of Aberdeen and Dundee be styled *Right Honourable* in the same manner as those of Edinburgh and Glasgow.[[28]](#cite_note-dca-2003-31)

The [Chairman of the London County Council](/wiki/List_of_chairmen_of_the_London_County_Council "List of chairmen of the London County Council") (LCC) was granted the style in 1935 as part of the celebrations of the [silver jubilee](/wiki/Silver_jubilee "Silver jubilee") of King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V").[[32]](#cite_note-35) The Chairman of the [Greater London Council](/wiki/Greater_London_Council "Greater London Council") (GLC), the body that replaced the LCC in 1965, was similarly granted the style[[33]](#cite_note-36) until the GLC was abolished in 1986.

*Right Honourable* is also used as a style by the [Lord Lyon King of Arms](/wiki/Lord_Lyon_King_of_Arms "Lord Lyon King of Arms") in office,[[34]](#cite_note-37)[[35]](#cite_note-38)[[36]](#cite_note-39) preceding his title rather than his personal name, as with other applications ex officio.

#### In the House of Commons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: In the House of Commons")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/House_of_Commons_debating_Brexit_deal_-_19_October_2019.jpg/330px-House_of_Commons_debating_Brexit_deal_-_19_October_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:House_of_Commons_debating_Brexit_deal_-_19_October_2019.jpg)

In the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"), members use *honourable* and *right honourable* when referring to one another.

In the chamber of the [House of Commons](/wiki/British_House_of_Commons "British House of Commons"), [members](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") are not permitted to address each other directly, nor to name other members, but must instead address the [speaker](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_House_of_Commons_(United_Kingdom) "Speaker of the House of Commons (United Kingdom)") and refer to other members indirectly. This practice is intended to enforce a polite tone to maintain order and good honour.[[37]](#cite_note-40) Only the occupant of the Chair addresses Members by name. Members generally refer to one another as "my [honourable](/wiki/The_Honourable "The Honourable") friend" if in the same [party](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of political parties in the United Kingdom"), and "the honourable gentleman/lady/member" otherwise. If needed, constituencies ("the honourable member for ...") or ministerial offices (e.g. "my right honourable friend the Prime Minister") can be used for clarity.[[d]](#cite_note-41)

Referring to one another as *honourable* is merely a courtesy used within the House, and is not a style used outside the chamber. However, when a member is in fact entitled to be styled *Right Honourable* (in practice always through membership of the Privy Council), they are referred to as such in the chamber. Further embellishments are traditionally applied to clergy (*reverend*), military officers (*gallant*) and barristers (*learned*), a practice recommended to be abolished following a 2010 report of the [Modernisation Committee](/wiki/Modernisation_Committee "Modernisation Committee"), but in practice continued.[[38]](#cite_note-42)[[39]](#cite_note-43) In summary:

* "Honourable" is used for members who are not privy counsellors.
* "Right honourable" is used for members who are privy counsellors.
* "(Right) honourable and reverend" may be used for clergy.[[40]](#cite_note-44)
* "(Right) honourable and gallant" may be used for military officers.[[41]](#cite_note-45)[[42]](#cite_note-46)
* "(Right) honourable and learned" may be used for [barristers](/wiki/Barrister "Barrister").[[43]](#cite_note-47)[[44]](#cite_note-48)

#### Collective entities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Collective entities")]

*Right Honourable* is added as a prefix to the name of various collective entities, including:

* "The Right Honourable the Lords Spiritual and Temporal in Parliament Assembled", i.e. the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords")
* "The Right Honourable the Lords of the Privy Council", i.e. the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Privy Council of the United Kingdom")
* "The Right Honourable the Lords Commissioners of the Admiralty", i.e. the former [Board of Admiralty](/wiki/Board_of_Admiralty "Board of Admiralty")

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Canada")]

Further information: [Canadian honorifics](/wiki/Canadian_honorifics "Canadian honorifics")

In Canada, occupants of only the three most senior public offices are styled as *Right Honourable* (*Le/La très honorable* in French). Formerly, this was by virtue of their appointment to the [Privy Council of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Privy Council of the United Kingdom"). However, Canadian appointments to the British Privy Council were ended by the government of [Lester Pearson](/wiki/Lester_Pearson "Lester Pearson"). Currently, individuals who hold, or have held, one of the following offices are awarded the style of *Right Honourable* for life:

* [Governor General of Canada](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada")
* [Prime Minister of Canada](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada")
* [Chief Justice of Canada](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_Canada "Chief Justice of Canada")

*The Right Honourable* is not to be confused with [*His* or *Her Excellency*](/wiki/Excellency "Excellency"), used by governors general during their term of office, or *[the Honourable](/wiki/The_Honourable "The Honourable")*, used only while in office (except in Nova Scotia, Alberta, Saskatchewan, and Ontario, where honorary members of the Executive Council enjoy the title permanently) by provincial [premiers](/wiki/Premier_(Canada) "Premier (Canada)") and cabinet ministers, and for life by [senators](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada") and members of the [King's Privy Council for Canada](/wiki/King%27s_Privy_Council_for_Canada "King's Privy Council for Canada") (chiefly cabinet ministers, as well as other figures such as party leaders or provincial premiers who may be appointed from time to time).

The title may also be granted for life by the governor general to eminent Canadians who have not held any of the offices that would otherwise entitle them to the style. This has been done on two occasions: to eight prominent political figures to mark the 125th anniversary of [Canadian Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") in 1992,[[e]](#cite_note-49) and to longtime Member of Parliament [Herb Gray](/wiki/Herb_Gray "Herb Gray") upon his retirement in 2002.

### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

Previously in New Zealand the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_New_Zealand "Prime Minister of New Zealand") and some other senior cabinet ministers were customarily appointed to the [Privy Council of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Privy Council of the United Kingdom") and thus styled *Right Honourable*.[[45]](#cite_note-NZGRtHon.-50)

[Helen Clark](/wiki/Helen_Clark "Helen Clark") did not recommend the appointment of any new privy counsellors during her tenure as Prime Minister, from 1999 to 2008. In 2009 it was announced that her successor, [John Key](/wiki/John_Key "John Key"), had decided not to make any further recommendations to [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") for appointments to the Privy Council.[[46]](#cite_note-51)

In August 2010, the [Queen of New Zealand](/wiki/Monarchy_of_New_Zealand "Monarchy of New Zealand") announced that, with immediate effect, individuals who hold, and those persons who after the date of the signing of these rules are appointed to, the following offices are awarded the style *Right Honourable* for life:[[45]](#cite_note-NZGRtHon.-50)

* the [Governor-General of New Zealand](/wiki/Governor-General_of_New_Zealand "Governor-General of New Zealand")
* the [Prime Minister of New Zealand](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_New_Zealand "Prime Minister of New Zealand")
* the [Chief Justice of New Zealand](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_New_Zealand "Chief Justice of New Zealand")
* the [Speaker of the New Zealand House of Representatives](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_New_Zealand_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the New Zealand House of Representatives")

This change was made because the practice of appointing New Zealanders to the Privy Council of the United Kingdom had ceased. However, the change had little immediate effect, as all but two of the holders or living former holders of the offices granted the style had already been appointed to the Privy Council.[[47]](#cite_note-52)[[48]](#cite_note-53)

The living New Zealanders holding the style *Right Honourable* as a result of membership of the Privy Council are:

* [Helen Elizabeth Clark](/wiki/Helen_Clark "Helen Clark") (1990) – prime minister

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Africa")]

During the periods of its existence, the [Prime Minister of Kenya](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Kenya "Prime Minister of Kenya") post was styled *Right Honourable*. In post-independence Kenya two people have been styled with the "right honorable" title. The country's founding president [Jomo Kenyatta](/wiki/Jomo_Kenyatta "Jomo Kenyatta") who was the first prime minister of Kenya and [Raila Amolo Odinga](/wiki/Raila_Amolo_Odinga "Raila Amolo Odinga"), who served as the country's second prime minister between 2008 and 2013. [Raila Odinga](/wiki/Raila_Odinga "Raila Odinga") also known as Baba (Political father and Enigma) is a towering icon in Africa, well known for his [Pan-Africanism](/wiki/Pan-Africanism "Pan-Africanism") and championing for good governance. The [prime ministers of Namibia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Namibia "Prime Minister of Namibia") and [Uganda](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Uganda "Prime Minister of Uganda") are both currently styled with the same honorific. The speaker and deputy speaker of the [Parliament of Uganda](/wiki/Parliament_of_Uganda "Parliament of Uganda") are also entitled to the style.

### Caribbean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Caribbean")]

The prime ministers of [Barbados](/wiki/Barbados "Barbados"), [Grenada](/wiki/Grenada "Grenada"), [Saint Lucia](/wiki/Saint_Lucia "Saint Lucia"), [Saint Vincent and the Grenadines](/wiki/Saint_Vincent_and_the_Grenadines "Saint Vincent and the Grenadines") and [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago") are styled as *Right Honourable*. The [West Indies Federation prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_West_Indies_Federation "Prime Minister of the West Indies Federation") was also styled as such during that office's short existence.

### Malaysia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Malaysia")]

In Malaysia, only the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia "Prime Minister of Malaysia"), his or her [deputy](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia "Deputy Prime Minister of Malaysia"), [four judges of the Federal Court](/wiki/Federal_Court_of_Malaysia "Federal Court of Malaysia")[[f]](#cite_note-54) and MPs who are titled [Tun](/wiki/Malay_styles_and_titles "Malay styles and titles") are styled as *Right Honourable* (Malay: Yang Amat Berhormat, Yang Amat Arif for judges) at the federal level. For the state level, all the [Menteris Besar, Chief Ministers and Premier](/wiki/Heads_of_state_governments_of_Malaysia "Heads of state governments of Malaysia") along with their deputies are also styled *Right Honourable*.

### Nepal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Nepal")]

In Nepal, the [president](/wiki/President_of_Nepal "President of Nepal"), [vice president](/wiki/Vice_President_of_Nepal "Vice President of Nepal") speaker of the House of Representatives, [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Nepal "Prime Minister of Nepal") and [chief justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_Nepal "Chief Justice of Nepal") are formally styled *Right Honourable* ([Nepali](/wiki/Nepali_language "Nepali language"): सम्माननीय, romanized: *Sammānanīya*). Ministers, members of parliament (Lower and Upper Houses and provincial parliaments) and Chief ministers of provinces are styled "Honourable" only. It is usually joked during informal discussions about the use of the word "Honourable" to differentiate senior and less senior government dignitories. It can also be spelled in English as *The Rt. Hon’ble*.

### South Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: South Korea")]

In [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), the [President](/wiki/President_of_South_Korea "President of South Korea"), [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_South_Korea "Prime Minister of South Korea"), [Speaker of the National Assembly](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_National_Assembly_of_South_Korea "Speaker of the National Assembly of South Korea") and [Chief Justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_Korea "Chief Justice of the Supreme Court of Korea") can use the *Right Honourable* style.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Spain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Spain")]

In the Spanish [autonomous community](/wiki/Autonomous_communities_of_Spain "Autonomous communities of Spain") of [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"), the [president of the Generalitat de Catalunya](/wiki/President_of_the_Government_of_Catalonia "President of the Government of Catalonia") and the president of the [Parliament of Catalonia](/wiki/Parliament_of_Catalonia "Parliament of Catalonia") are formally referred as the *Right Honourable*.

## Countries with rare or historic usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Countries with rare or historic usage")]

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Australia")]

In Australia, the [lord mayors](/wiki/Lord_mayor "Lord mayor") of [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide"), [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), [Hobart](/wiki/Hobart "Hobart"), [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth") and [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") are entitled to be styled *Right Honourable* while in office.

Historically, a number of Australians were entitled to the style as members of the [Privy Council of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Privy Council of the United Kingdom"). Appointment to the Australian equivalent of the Privy Council, the [Federal Executive Council](/wiki/Federal_Executive_Council_(Australia) "Federal Executive Council (Australia)"), does not entitle a person to the style. Typical appointees to the Imperial Privy Council included senior politicians and judges at state and federal level. [Malcolm Fraser](/wiki/Malcolm_Fraser "Malcolm Fraser") in 1976 was the most recent prime minister to accept appointment to the Privy Council and thus to be styled *Right Honourable*. Of his 21 predecessors, only four were not members of the Privy Council – [Alfred Deakin](/wiki/Alfred_Deakin "Alfred Deakin") (declined appointment), [Chris Watson](/wiki/Chris_Watson "Chris Watson") (never offered), [Arthur Fadden](/wiki/Arthur_Fadden "Arthur Fadden") (accepted after leaving office), and [Gough Whitlam](/wiki/Gough_Whitlam "Gough Whitlam") (declined appointment). The last Governor-General to be entitled to the style was [Sir Ninian Stephen](/wiki/Ninian_Stephen "Ninian Stephen"), who left office in 1988. The last active politician to be entitled to the style was [Ian Sinclair](/wiki/Ian_Sinclair "Ian Sinclair"), who retired in 1998. The few Australian recipients of British peerages were also entitled to the style.

Present-day Australian governments no longer recommend Australians for elevation to the peerage or appointment to the Privy Council. However, some present-day Australian citizens either hold hereditary peerages (e.g. [Malcolm Murray, 12th Earl of Dunmore](/wiki/Malcolm_Murray,_12th_Earl_of_Dunmore "Malcolm Murray, 12th Earl of Dunmore")) or obtained the style another way (e.g. [Ian Sinclair](/wiki/Ian_Sinclair "Ian Sinclair")).

### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Ireland")]

Members of the [Privy Council of Ireland](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_Ireland "Privy Council of Ireland") were entitled to be styled *Right Honourable*, even after the Privy Council ceased to have any functions or to meet on the creation of the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") in December 1922. Nevertheless, the [Lord Mayor of Dublin](/wiki/Lord_Mayor_of_Dublin "Lord Mayor of Dublin"), like some of his counterparts in Great Britain, retained the use of the honorific style as a result of its having been conferred separately by legislation; in 2001 it was removed, as a consequence of [local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") law reform.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Sri Lanka

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Sri Lanka")]

In [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") (formerly known as [Ceylon](/wiki/British_Ceylon "British Ceylon")) the British practice was followed with Ceylonese members of the [Privy Council of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Privy Council of the United Kingdom") were styled *Right Honourable* and were referred to as *Mahamanya* in [Sinhala](/wiki/Sinhala_language "Sinhala language"). Ceylonese appointees to the privy council included [D. S. Senanayake](/wiki/D._S._Senanayake "D. S. Senanayake") and [Sir John Kotelawala](/wiki/John_Kotelawala "John Kotelawala").[[49]](#cite_note-55)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Right_Honourable&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** This is generally the case for all similar adjectival styles (e.g. *[The Reverend](/wiki/The_Reverend "The Reverend")*, *[The Right Excellent](/wiki/The_Right_Excellent "The Right Excellent")*). In contrast, styles in the form of a noun (e.g. *[Majesty](/wiki/Majesty "Majesty")*, *[Holiness](/wiki/Holiness "Holiness")*) can be used with the corresponding possessive pronoun in direct address or in place of a name.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** Black's *Titles and Forms of Address* claims[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlack195544-15) that the capitalised *The* before substantive peers' titles is an abbreviated form of their style. For example, "The Earl of Beaconsfield" would be short for "The Right Honourable Earl of Beaconsfield". However, this argument does not account for the second *The* in commonly-found formulations of the kind "The Right Honourable The Earl of Beaconsfield"[[15]](#cite_note-artuk-beaconsfield-16) or "The Most Honourable The Marquess of Salisbury"[[16]](#cite_note-hankey-1936-17); and the argument is not corroborated by other guides which recommend the latter forms.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKiddWilliamson1985''passim''-18)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHickey2013396–402-19)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMontague-Smith197921–37-20)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-29)** This practice is recommended by *Debrett's Peerage and Baronetage*[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKiddWilliamson198543headings_"Privy_Counsellors"_and_"Rt_Honourable"-22)[[21]](#cite_note-DebrettPC-23)[[22]](#cite_note-Debretts-24) and *Correct Form*,[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMontague-Smith197928,_35,_80–82-25) and Hickey's *Honor and Respect*;[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHickey2013365–366-26) and is applied consistently throughout *Burke's Peerage & Baronetage*.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMosley1999''passim''-27) However, it is considered incorrect by Black's *Titles and Forms of Address*, which holds that membership of the Privy Council results in no post-nominal letters because it is an office and not an honour.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlack195544,_101–102-28)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-41)** In [Hansard](/wiki/Hansard "Hansard"), references to other MPs are expanded to include a constituency or ministerial office (and name in parentheses) as an aid to readers, whether or not this is what was actually said. *Honourable* is presently always abbreviated to *hon*.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-49)** [Martial Asselin](/wiki/Martial_Asselin "Martial Asselin"), [Ellen Fairclough](/wiki/Ellen_Fairclough "Ellen Fairclough"), [Alvin Hamilton](/wiki/Alvin_Hamilton "Alvin Hamilton"), [Paul Martin Sr.](/wiki/Paul_Martin_Sr. "Paul Martin Sr."), [Don Mazankowski](/wiki/Don_Mazankowski "Don Mazankowski"), [Jean-Luc Pépin](/wiki/Jean-Luc_P%C3%A9pin "Jean-Luc Pépin"), [Jack Pickersgill](/wiki/Jack_Pickersgill "Jack Pickersgill"), and [Robert Stanfield](/wiki/Robert_Stanfield "Robert Stanfield").
6. **[^](#cite_ref-54)** The four judges are the Chief Justice, the President of the Court of Appeal, and the Chief Judges of the High Court in Malaya and the High Court in Sabah and Sarawak
